skill.name: systematic-debugging
skill.description: Guides AI coding agents through root cause investigation before proposing or applying bug fixes.
skill.summary: A structured debugging workflow for tracing failures, testing hypotheses, and fixing root causes.
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives from markdown diagrams, TypeScript examples, timeout strings, and debugging snippets. The real concerns are operational: the included shell helper runs project tests, and diagnostic examples can expose local environment or keychain metadata if used carelessly.

medium_findings.title: Executable Test Bisection Script Runs Project Tests
medium_findings.description: The helper script accepts a check path and test pattern, enumerates matching files, and runs npm test for each file. This is legitimate debugging behavior, but running tests in an untrusted project can execute arbitrary package scripts or test code.
medium_findings.recommendation: Publish with a warning that users should inspect projects and package scripts before running the helper on untrusted code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script clearly executes npm test against discovered files. Intent is debugging, but the execution risk is real in untrusted repositories.

medium_findings.title: Diagnostic Examples Can Expose Local Secrets or Identity Metadata
medium_findings.description: The skill shows diagnostic commands for environment propagation, macOS keychain identity listing, and signing. These examples do not exfiltrate data, but copied output could reveal sensitive environment or identity metadata in logs or chats.
medium_findings.recommendation: Warn users to redact secrets, signing identities, stack traces, and environment data before sharing diagnostic output.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are normal debugging diagnostics, but they intentionally inspect environment and identity state. Risk depends on how users handle the output.

low_findings.title: Markdown Diagram Blocks Triggered External Command False Positives
low_findings.description: Several static findings point to fenced dot and TypeScript examples rather than executable skill logic. These blocks explain debugging decisions and are not invoked by the skill itself.
low_findings.recommendation: No security change required for these documentation blocks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are fenced documentation examples. No automatic command execution path is present in those files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Timeout and Date Examples
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography hits correspond to Date.now timeout checks, timeout error messages, and debugging prose. No hashing, encryption, signing algorithm selection, or credential handling code is present at those lines.
low_findings.recommendation: No cryptography remediation is needed for these timeout examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code uses Date.now for elapsed time in polling helpers. This is unrelated to cryptographic randomness or algorithms.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Findings Are Local Validation or Polluter Checks
low_findings.description: Filesystem access is used to demonstrate directory validation, wait-for-file polling, or checking whether test pollution appeared. These are local debugging patterns, not data theft or destructive file operations.
low_findings.recommendation: Keep filesystem examples scoped to local debugging and avoid running helpers outside intended projects.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are checks and validation examples. I found no deletion, overwrite, collection, or network transfer behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Command Execution Through npm test
dangerous_patterns.description: The shell helper launches npm test for each discovered test file. This is useful for finding polluting tests, but package scripts can execute arbitrary local code.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Run only in repositories you trust, or inspect package scripts first.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command execution is explicit and local. The risk is bounded by user intent and project trust.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive Diagnostic Output Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Example diagnostics inspect environment values, keychain identities, current working directory, and stack traces. These are not malicious, but users may leak information if they paste raw output into shared channels.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Redact secrets, identity names, paths, and stack details before sharing output.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The diagnostics deliberately reveal local context. I found no exfiltration mechanism or hidden automation.

content.value_statement: Random fixes waste time and often create new failures. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a root cause workflow before any fix is proposed.
actual_capabilities: Guides root cause investigation before code changes.
Separates symptoms, hypotheses, evidence, and fixes.
Provides checklists for test failures, production bugs, and build issues.
Shows how to trace bad values backward through call chains.
Recommends condition-based waits for flaky asynchronous tests.
Includes a shell helper for finding tests that create unwanted state.
limitations: It does not diagnose bugs automatically without project evidence.
It cannot guarantee a fix when systems are external or unavailable.
The helper script assumes an npm test workflow.
Some diagnostic examples may need redaction before sharing output.

use_cases.title: Stabilize Flaky Tests
use_cases.description: Replace guess-based sleeps with condition checks and trace the real timing dependency.

use_cases.title: Triage Production Failures
use_cases.description: Gather evidence, check recent changes, and avoid risky quick fixes during incidents.

use_cases.title: Review Failed Fix Attempts
use_cases.description: Stop repeated patching and decide whether the root problem is architectural.

prompt_templates.title: Start Debugging
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for any new bug.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use systematic debugging for this bug. First list the error, reproduction steps, recent changes, and evidence you need before suggesting fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Trace A Failure
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt for a deep stack error.
prompt_templates.prompt: Trace this failure backward from the symptom. Identify each caller, the bad value at each layer, and the first place the value becomes wrong.

prompt_templates.title: Investigate Flaky Tests
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt for async or timing issues.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the condition-based waiting guidance. Find every arbitrary timeout, name the condition it hides, and propose evidence-gathering before changes.

prompt_templates.title: Recover After Failed Fixes
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt after several failed attempts.
prompt_templates.prompt: We tried multiple fixes and the bug remains. Stop implementation, summarize failed hypotheses, identify architectural assumptions, and propose the next investigation step.

output_examples.input: A payment test sometimes receives pending instead of completed.
output: The likely symptom is a race around status propagation.
The next step is to trace the status update path and replace sleeps with a condition check.
No fix should be applied until the missing transition is observed.

output_examples.input: A git command runs in the source directory during tests.
output: The investigation should start at the command location and trace the directory value backward.
The report should name where the empty or wrong directory first appears.
The fix should be applied at the source and guarded at later layers.

output_examples.input: A first fix failed and created a second failure.
output: The process should stop new patching immediately.
The failed hypothesis should be recorded with the evidence that disproved it.
A new investigation should check whether the design assumption is wrong.

best_practices: Collect reproducible evidence before proposing code changes.
Change one variable at a time when testing a hypothesis.
Redact environment, identity, and stack trace details before sharing logs.
anti_patterns: Adding retries, sleeps, or guards before understanding the failure.
Bundling refactors with a bug fix during investigation.
Continuing to patch after several failed hypotheses without questioning design.

faq.question: Does this skill fix bugs automatically?
faq.answer: No. It structures investigation so an AI assistant can find root cause before proposing a fix.

faq.question: Can I use it during urgent incidents?
faq.answer: Yes. It helps gather the minimum evidence needed to avoid risky symptom fixes under pressure.

faq.question: Does it support flaky test debugging?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes condition-based waiting guidance for asynchronous and timing-dependent tests.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes a shell helper and diagnostic commands. They are legitimate but should be run with care.

faq.question: Does it send data over the network?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The reviewed files do not contain network exfiltration behavior.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
